However, a public official is not required to obtain a professional appraisal to
assist in assessing the financial effect of a decision for confli.tof-irt"r"rt fri.po."r.
(Condon Advice Letter, No. I-02-035; McHughAdvice Letter,No. 4-02-0 2l; HoAdvice

As previously advised, in your situation the question that must be asked is if it is
reasonably foreseeable that the governmental decision will: (1) Result in an increase or
decrease in the business entity's or source of income's gross revenues for a fiscal year in
the amount of $20,000 or more; or (2) The governmentll decision will result in the
business entity incurring or avoiding additional expenses or reducing or eliminating
existing expenses for a fiscal year in the amount oi$s,000 or more; or (3) The
goverrmental decision will result in an increase or decrease in the value of the business
entity's assets or liabilities of $20,000 or more. (Regulation 1g705.1((c)(a)(A)_(c).)
If you determine that the underlying facts upon which the appraisal is based are
correct and that the opinion is a reasonable one based on consideration of all relevant
facts, your reliance on the appraisal constitutes a good faith determination and you may
pariicipate in the decision. (see oderman Advici Letter,No. a_oo_ogi.,

sections.question_synthetic: Whether a public official (Vice-Mayor Ray Luna) can participate in a governmental decision regarding a proposed apartment building development on land that includes a property from which he conducts a real estate business, and whether obtaining a professional appraisal satisfies conflict-of-interest analysis under the Political Reform Act.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: A public official may rely on a professional appraisal as a good faith effort to assess financial effects of a decision on economic interests, provided the appraisal is based on accurate facts and considers statutory materiality factors. The FPPC cannot evaluate factual accuracy of appraisals but confirms that obtaining an appraisal is not required; ultimately, the official must determine whether the facts support the appraisal and whether material financial effects exist under the three-prong materiality test ($20,000+ gross revenue impact, $5,000+ expense impact, or $20,000+ asset value impact).
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Informal advice letter (File No. I-06-212) with no formal QUESTION or CONCLUSION sections; extracted question and conclusion from letter body. Note states informal assistance does not provide immunity of formal opinion. References multiple prior advice letters for precedent.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Whether a public official (Vice-Mayor Ray Luna) can participate in a governmental decision regarding a proposed apartment building development on land that includes a property from which he conducts a real estate business, and whether obtaining a professional appraisal satisfies conflict-of-interest analysis under the Political Reform Act.

CONCLUSION: A public official may rely on a professional appraisal as a good faith effort to assess financial effects of a decision on economic interests, provided the appraisal is based on accurate facts and considers statutory materiality factors. The FPPC cannot evaluate factual accuracy of appraisals but confirms that obtaining an appraisal is not required; ultimately, the official must determine whether the facts support the appraisal and whether material financial effects exist under the three-prong materiality test ($20,000+ gross revenue impact, $5,000+ expense impact, or $20,000+ asset value impact).

embedding.summary: FPPC responds to Vice-Mayor Luna's informal assistance request regarding conflict-of-interest analysis for a proposed apartment development on property containing his real estate business. The commission advises that while obtaining a professional appraisal constitutes good faith effort to assess financial effects, it is not required, and the official must ultimately determine whether material financial effects trigger participation restrictions.
